text: The video shows the hernia from the abdominal portion, with visible protrusion of the stomach through the diaphragm into the thoracic cavity.

The surgeon begins the dissection by mobilizing the gastropatic ligament up to the level of the right crus. The hernia sac is grasped and reduced within an avascular plane using a combination of blunt dissection and harmonic scalpel dissection, ensuring minimal bleeding.
The hernia sac is gradually reduced into the abdominal cavity, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects at the base of the crura to identify the decussation, followed by the division of the short gastric vessels up to the level of the left crus.
The surgeon observes the elongated short gastric vessels in the patient's abdomen, a result of the chronic and large hernia.
The surgeon passes a Penrose drain behind the esophagus to aid in retraction, exposing both the left and right crura and cleaning up the decussation.

The surgeon performs medi-central dissection of the esophagus using blunt dissection and a harmonic scalpel, extending the dissection up to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins. This mobilizes the esophagus intra-abdominally by approximately three to four centimeters, ensuring a tension-free repair.

The surgical view shows the esophagus positioned below, with the pericardium visible at the top of the screen. The dissection progresses to the level of the inferior pulmonary veins, first revealing the left inferior pulmonary vein, followed by the right inferior pulmonary vein.
The surgeon demonstrates the intra-abdominal esophageal length, followed by performing a corral repair using O-ethabon sutures.
The surgeon places lateral sutures to re-approximate the hiatus, addressing the anatomical challenges presented by the patient's chronic and large hernia.
The surgeon passes the fundus of the stomach posterior to the esophagus and performs the classic shoe shine maneuver, visibly wrapping the stomach around the esophagus.
The surgeon tacks the fundus of the stomach to the diaphragm on both sides using two O-ethabon sutures, setting up the Toupee fundoplication.
The surgeon places a total of four sutures from the esophagus to the stomach, with two sutures on each side, securing the fundoplication.
